The hand of God in this chapter moves with a quietness that demands your attention. A Hebrew mother places her newborn son in a basket on a river controlled by a king bent on destroying him—an act of faith so desperate it seems mad. Yet within hours, Pharaoh's own daughter draws the child from the water and raises him in the palace, and his own mother becomes his nurse, paid by the very household that sought his death. This is not luck or coincidence. This is God working through the ordinary choices of frightened people, bending the will of the powerful toward His purposes without their knowing it. The daughter of Pharaoh does not recognize she is preserving the one who will bring her father's kingdom to ruin. She acts from compassion for a crying child. And in that small mercy, God's covenant promise moves forward.

But then the chapter shifts, and you encounter the adult Moses—and he is broken in ways that matter. When he sees an Egyptian beating a Hebrew, something ignites in him. He looks around to ensure no one is watching, then kills the man and buries the body. This is not deliverance; this is murder wrapped in rage and self-protection. The very next day, when two Hebrews quarrel, one of them throws Moses's violence back in his face: "Who made you ruler and judge over us?" The question cuts deep because Moses has no answer. He is not yet the deliverer God will make him. He is a man of divided loyalties, raised in Pharaoh's house but called by blood to his own people, belonging fully to neither. His attempt to save one Hebrew exposes the truth—he cannot save anyone. He can only run.

So Moses flees to Midian, and here too God works through the texture of ordinary life. He sits by a well, exhausted and exiled, and encounters the daughters of a priest. When shepherds drive them away, Moses helps them—not as Israel's liberator, but simply as a man who sees injustice and acts. He marries one of them, Zipporah, and settles into a quiet life as a foreigner in a foreign land, naming his son Gershom to mark his displacement. Years pass. The king who hunted him dies. And in that silence, something crucial happens: the Israelites groan under their bondage, and their cry rises to God. God hears them. God remembers His covenant. God sees them and takes notice. The chapter ends not with Moses—who remains in Midian, unaware—but with God's attention turning toward His enslaved people. Moses had to fail so that you would understand this truth: deliverance comes not from a man's strength or cunning, but from God's faithfulness to His word and His people's cry.

This is where your trust must settle. You live in a world where injustice appears unended, where your own attempts to fix things often create new wreckage, where you feel like a foreigner in a land that was supposed to be home. But God hears the cry that rises from real suffering. He does not forget His covenant. He sees you. And He is moving through the ordinary details of your life—through the small mercies of others, through your failures that humble you, through your displacement that teaches you dependence—to accomplish what you cannot accomplish yourself. The question is not whether God will act. The question is whether you will trust His timing and His way, even when the deliverer He is preparing seems nowhere in sight.
